titleEn: Indiana Livestock Summit focuses on collaboration, innovation
titleZh: 印第安纳州畜牧业峰会聚焦协作与创新
summaryZh: 印第安纳州畜牧业峰会于2026年6月30日举行，主题为协作、创新、多元化和创业精神。州长Mike Braun强调农业对经济的重要性，并宣布10亿美元生命科学计划。峰会讨论了为养猪业吸引顶尖人才、开发AI监测系统观察猪只行为等议题。会议指出，过去30年通过创新，肉类和牛奶产量增长50%，鸡蛋产量增长30%，猪肉产量增长50%，而饲料用量仅增加20%，体现了效率与可持续性。
featuredReason: 该新闻展示了畜牧业与种植业协作的宏观趋势，并提及AI监测系统在猪只行为观察中的应用，对智慧畜牧从业者了解政策环境与跨行业合作有参考价值。
contentFull: Livestock ManagementFeedPork Market NewsHog WelfareNewsIndiana Livestock Summit focuses on collaboration, innovationTopics included capturing top talent for future careers in the pork industry; developing an AI monitoring system to observe swine behavior.Industry ReleaseJune 30, 2026schedule6 Min ReadIndiana Corn and SoyCorn and soybean farmers share many things in common with beef, pork, dairy and poultry producers. That’s why the Indiana Soybean Alliance’s Membership and Policy Committee and the Indiana Corn Growers Association recently hosted the Livestock Summit at the JW Marriott hotel in Indianapolis.The summit began with a chat between Indiana Gov. Mike Braun and Courtney Kingery, CEO of ISA and ICGA, and continued with presentations from several ag stakeholders throughout the day. The theme was working together and implementing innovation, diversification and entrepreneurship.“Over and over during the summit we heard about collaboration across the entire animal ag value chain,” Kingery said. “Gov. Braun summed it up by touting innovation, diversification and entrepreneurship. Through those principles during the past 30 years, we have realized a 50% increase in meat and milk production, a 30% increase in egg production, pork production is up 50% all while feed usage has only increased by 20%. That’s a great story of efficiency, sustainability and innovation.”Related:Texas seeks FDA approval for feed-based ivermectinGov. Braun said the state’s agriculture sector is crucial to Indiana’s economy. “The valuable role of agriculture in Indiana’s economy cannot be overstated,” Braun said. “It comes into play in every one of our counties. I’m committed to ensuring the future of this foundational industry is bright. That’s why I launched our state’s $1 billion life sciences initiative, which, in part, will ensure Indiana’s agriculture continues to feed, clothe and fuel America.”Gov. Braun’s administration has promoted nutritional benefits through its Making Indiana Healthy Again efforts. This initiative may also include opportunities for Indiana’s farmers. “Indiana-raised pork, eggs, dairy, poultry and beef are some of the most nutritious foods in the world, and I think Make Indiana Healthy Again is an opportunity for agriculture to help write that story,” Braun said. “Healthy should be defined by nutrition, not by farm size or production model. Make Indiana Healthy Again includes pathways to get more, healthy Indiana produce into Hoosier homes and schools. My administration wants to be a partner with producers.”Promoting collaborationThe summit was focused on the collaborative efforts shaping the animal agriculture and feed industries. That collaboration strengthens the value chain to support corn and soy farmers and livestock producers.“More than half of Indiana’s corn and soybean production goes toward feeding livestock,” said ICGA President Alan Dunn, a farmer from Michigantown, Indiana. “That statistic alone tells an important story, namely, what is good for one is also good for the other. When row crop production is successful, we have a safe, reliable, cost-effective food source for our livestock partners. When livestock producers do well, we have additional demand for the corn and soybeans we produce.”Related:Strengthen FAD preparedness: 3 essential steps for pork producersDavid Hardin is the chair of ISA’s Membership and Policy Committee and a farmer from Avon, Indiana. In addition to growing soybeans and corn, he is also a pork producer who has served on the Indiana Pork Producers Association board and the Indiana Board of Animal Health. Hardin understands the need for collaboration from both sides of the farm fence.“Agriculture faces a lot of challenges; I think that if we face them with a unified front, we will be able to withstand anything that’s thrown at us,” Hardin said. “This summit is an opportunity to have honest conversations about those challenges and opportunities that we’re going to see ahead. I want to thank you for being here and being part of that conversation. I hope that these discussions help prepare us as an organization to advocate better for all of you.”Discussing the detailsThe summit’s speakers and panel discussions covered the tactics improving farm profitability. For many years, farmers have relied on export markets to increase crop values. Megan Bixler is the International Trade Director for the Indiana State Department of Agriculture. Prior to a panel discussion on growing export markets, Bixler said farm exports remain strong.Related:Improved parity distribution may be biggest benefit of adopting skip-a-heatShe stated, “In 2025, the U.S. exported $171 billion worth of U.S. agricultural products. That’s slightly down from the peak that we hit in 2022. That was a record at nearly $196 billion. This decline is mostly due to returning to normal levels after that post-COVID boom, strengthening U.S. dollar inflation and increased competition for key products.”Kingery moderated a discussion on ag export opportunities that featured Ryan LeGrand of the U.S. Grain and BioProducts Council, John Hinners of the U.S. Meat Export Federation, Allison Thomas of the U.S. Dairy Export Council, and Shelby Watson of the USA Poultry and Egg Export Council. The panel concluded that global demand, evolving trade policy and expanding market access continue to shape the future of American agriculture.Aaron Kuhn, a regional marketing manager for ethanol producer POET, led a panel discussion titled Feed Supply Resilience and Innovation. The experts included Trent Torrance of United Animal Health, Kristy Lowe of Lowe’s Pellets and Grain, and Charles Shininger of CSA Animal Nutrition. The panel said feed availability, input costs and supply chain reliability continue to play a vital role in the success of livestock operations across the country.“There’s tremendous opportunity for livestock producers over the next 10 years when you talk about protein demand, innovation and feeding the world,” Torrance said. “I think when we look at challenges, we see the negative in that challenge. But there’s also a lot of opportunity in that. When we look at the global supply chain, it’s more difficult due to shipping problems around the world. But when we look at the demand for some of the ingredients, it’s also an opportunity to look inward and solve some of those other problems. So, I think there’s always going to be an opportunity to produce protein more efficiently, to feed the world more efficiently, and make the world healthier.”Navigating regulationsThe summit’s programming also covered government regulations and how the livestock industry adjusts. Indiana State Poultry Association President Rebecca Eifert Joniskan led a panel discussion about how food safety and environmental regulations affect poultry producers.The panel included Kim Rice of Rose Acre Farms, Jason Glenn of Maple Leaf Farms and Kate Borders of Miller Poultry. The group concluded that consumer expectations are driving regulations for chicken, turkey and duck producers.Elanco Animal Health Executive Vice President Jose Simas and Kingery moderated a discussion about changes in animal health and how livestock producers respond. The leaders in the program were Indiana pork producer Brian Martin, David Dixon of the Indiana Beef Cattlemen’s Association, Phil Seger of turkey producer Farbest Foods, and southern Indiana dairy producer Sam Schwoeppe. The livestock leaders determined that innovations in animal health, evolving regulations and critical partnerships with corn and soybean farmers support a strong and sustainable food system.Other featured speakers were:Indiana Farm Bureau Senior Director of Government Affairs Ryan Hoff covered the state’s process of assessing property for tax purposes.Chad Martin of Indiana Pork and Byron Ernest of Leadery Global talked about a program to capture top talent from Indiana high schools for future careers in the pork industry.Michigan State University Researcher Daniel Morris explained his efforts to develop an artificial intelligence monitoring system to constantly observe swine behavior.Brian Rexing of Dairy Farmers of America and the National Milk Producers Federation discussed farm-to-food systems including labor, nutrition and producer collaborations.The event’s sponsors included Alliant, American Dairy Association Indiana, Farm Credit Mid-America, Corteva Agriscience, Indiana Beef Cattle Association, U.S. Dairy Export Council, Indiana Corn Marketing Council, Indiana Dairy Producers, CSA Animal Nutrition, Indiana Farm Bureau, Indiana Pork, Indiana Soybean Alliance, Rose Acre Farms, Agribusiness Council of Indiana, Indiana State Poultry Association, Lowe’s Pellets and Grain, POET, Quality Roasting, United Animal Health and the U.S.A. Poultry and Egg Export Council.About the AuthorIndustry ReleaseSee more from Industry Releasechevron_rightSubscribe to Our NewslettersNational Hog Farmer is the source for hog production, management and market newsSIGN ME UPYou May Also LikeSponsoredWhat is the Arginine Requirement – Well It DependsJul 01, 2026Farming Business ManagementDecade of decline: EU pig population down nearly 9%by National Hog Farmer StaffJun 30, 2026Farm Policy NewsMarshall backs away from California Prop 12 fightby Ann HessJun 12, 2026Livestock ManagementWean-to-finish mortality continues to burden the industryby Rachel Johnson, MetaFarmsMay 06, 2026RecommendedLivestock ManagementSoybean meal's hidden valueJun 26, 2026SponsoredWhat is the Arginine Requirement – Well It DependsJul 01, 2026Farm Policy NewsSCOTUS refuses to hear pork producers' Q3 challengeJul 01, 2026Livestock ManagementTexas seeks FDA approval for feed-based ivermectinJul 02, 2026Google Preferred SourceSet National Hog Farmer as your "preferred source" to get quicker access to the news you value.Add Us NowRecent HeadlinesLivestock ManagementTexas seeks FDA approval for feed-based ivermectinTexas seeks FDA approval for feed-based iver
translationZh:
印第安纳州玉米和大豆种植者与牛肉、猪肉、乳制品和家禽生产者有许多共同点。这就是为什么印第安纳大豆联盟的会员与政策委员会以及印第安纳玉米种植者协会最近在印第安纳波利斯的JW万豪酒店举办了畜牧业峰会。

峰会以印第安纳州州长Mike Braun与ISA和ICGA首席执行官Courtney Kingery之间的对话开始，随后全天有多位农业利益相关者进行了演讲。主题是共同努力，实施创新、多元化和创业精神。

“在峰会期间，我们一再听到整个动物农业价值链上的协作，”Kingery说。“Braun州长通过倡导创新、多元化和创业精神进行了总结。通过过去30年的这些原则，我们实现了肉类和牛奶产量增长50%，鸡蛋产量增长30%，猪肉产量增长50%，而饲料用量仅增加了20%。这是一个关于效率、可持续性和创新的伟大故事。”

相关：德克萨斯州寻求FDA批准饲料用伊维菌素

Braun州长表示，该州的农业部门对印第安纳州的经济至关重要。“农业在印第安纳州经济中的宝贵作用怎么强调都不为过，”Braun说。“它在我们每个县都发挥着作用。我致力于确保这一基础产业的未来光明。这就是为什么我启动了该州10亿美元的生命科学计划，该计划将部分确保印第安纳州的农业继续为美国提供食物、衣物和燃料。”

Braun州长的政府通过其“让印第安纳州再次健康”的努力推广了营养益处。这一倡议也可能为印第安纳州的农民带来机会。“印第安纳州养殖的猪肉、鸡蛋、乳制品、家禽和牛肉是世界上最营养的食物之一，我认为‘让印第安纳州再次健康’是农业帮助书写这个故事的机会，”Braun说。“健康应该由营养来定义，而不是由农场规模或生产模式来定义。‘让印第安纳州再次健康’包括让更多健康的印第安纳州农产品进入印第安纳州家庭和学校的途径。我的政府希望成为生产者的合作伙伴。”

**促进协作**

峰会的重点是塑造动物农业和饲料行业的协作努力。这种协作加强了价值链，以支持玉米和大豆种植者以及畜牧生产者。

“印第安纳州一半以上的玉米和大豆产量用于饲养牲畜，”印第安纳州玉米种植者协会主席、来自印第安纳州米奇根镇的农民Alan Dunn说。“仅这一统计数据就讲述了一个重要的故事，即
contentHtml: Topics included capturing top talent for future careers in the pork industry; developing an AI monitoring system to observe swine behavior.
